titel: Increased opportunities for home charging of electric vehicles.
html:
The proposal includes suggestions aimed at enabling more people to charge their electric vehicles near their homes.

The proposals stipulate that a tenant or homeowner should have the right to request the installation of a charging point for charging an electric vehicle in the parking space associated with their residence, provided the parking space is located in the same building as the apartment or in close proximity to it. The resident will be responsible for the costs associated with the charging point. It should only be possible to refuse the installation if there is a valid reason. The proposals are based on an EU directive.

The proposed amendments to the law are scheduled to come into effect on May 29, 2026.

Proposal for Parliamentary Decision

Prop. 2025/26:148

Government's proposal:

  1. The Parliament adopts the government's proposal for an amendment to the Land Code.
  2. The Parliament adopts the government's proposal for an amendment to the Condominium Act (1991:614).
  3. The Parliament adopts the government's proposal for an amendment to the Act (1973:188) on Rental Boards and Housing Boards.

3

Prop. 2025/26:148 2
tldr: It will become easier for people who live in apartments or condominiums to install an electric vehicle charging station at their parking space. The proposed regulations will come into effect on May 29, 2026.
lattlast:
The government has proposed changes to several laws, including the Land Code and the Condominium Act. The purpose is to enable more people to charge their electric vehicles near their homes. This proposal originates from the EU and is called an EU directive.

If you live in an apartment and have a parking space in the same building or nearby, you should have the right to request the installation of a charging point. This means that your landlord or homeowners' association cannot refuse without a very good reason.

It is important to know that the person who wants the charger must pay for all costs incurred during its installation. Therefore, the landlord or homeowners' association is not responsible for the bill.

The new rules are planned to come into effect on May 29, 2026. The Parliament must vote on the proposal before it becomes a new law.
